title: How I Approach UX Research as a Student Designer
excerpt: A practical framework for conducting meaningful user research without a big budget or research team.
author: Apoorv Patel
content:
User research doesn't require expensive tools or large sample sizes to be valuable. As a student designer, I've learned that even 5-8 thoughtful interviews can uncover patterns that reshape entire product directions.

My process starts with defining clear research questions: What problem are we solving? Who experiences it most? What does success look like? From there, I recruit participants from my immediate network—classmates, friends, and campus communities—ensuring diversity in perspectives.

During interviews, I focus on past behavior rather than hypothetical preferences. 'Tell me about the last time you...' reveals more than 'Would you use...' ever could. I synthesize findings into affinity maps, identifying recurring themes and emotional triggers.

The key insight: research is not a phase you complete—it's a lens you carry through every design decision. Every wireframe, every color choice, every interaction should trace back to something a real user told you.
